original_hypothesis.title: Missing delegate/undelegate Functions Render VotingEscrow Delegation Architecture Dead Code
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The VotingEscrow contract architecturally supports delegation through LockedBalance.delegatee and LockedBalance.delegated fields, LockAction.DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE enum values, and an explicit delegation code path in increaseAmount. However, no external delegate() or undelegate() functions are implemented. Users can never change their delegatee from self, meaning the entire delegation subsystem is dead code. The QUIT lock action also has no corresponding function, leaving users with no early exit mechanism other than the global unlockOverride.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Multiple code paths handle delegation logic that can never execute. The missing functions represent a significant feature gap — users expected to delegate voting power have no mechanism to do so, and no individual early exit exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LockAction enum includes DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, QUIT with no corresponding external functions; LockedBalance has delegatee/delegated fields; increaseAmount has delegation branch at lines 271-281

preconditions: User wants to delegate voting power or exit lock early

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: VotingEscrow.sol lines 63-69 (enum) and lines 271-281 (delegation branch in increaseAmount)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dead code: delegation branch in increaseAmount is unreachable because delegatee always equals msg.sender; LockAction.DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE are defined but unused
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Protocol cannot support vote delegation despite architectural design; users locked for 5 years with no individual exit; dead code increases attack surface and audit complexity

analysis_questions: Was delegation intentionally removed or accidentally omitted?
Does the dead delegation code path in increaseAmount have correctness issues?
Should the LockedBalance struct be simplified if delegation is not supported?
validation_requirements: Confirm no function changes locked[].delegatee from msg.sender
Verify increaseAmount delegation branch is unreachable
Assess if dead code creates latent bugs if delegation is later added

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users expecting to delegate their veCANTO voting power to governance representatives or DAOs cannot do so. Users locked for 5 years have no individual early exit mechanism. The entire delegation economics designed into the contract are non-functional.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users cannot delegate, reducing governance participation efficiency; 5-year lock with no exit creates liquidity risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or new deployment

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test coverage for delegation or early exit scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately discoverable by users trying to delegate

validation_result.impact_summary: The VotingEscrow contract contains a partially implemented delegation subsystem that is entirely non-functional. Dead code paths in increaseAmount can never execute, and users have no delegation or early exit capabilities despite the architecture supporting them.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis accurately identifies that VotingEscrow.sol contains an incomplete delegation subsystem. The LockedBalance struct has `delegatee` and `delegated` fields, the LockAction enum includes DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, and QUIT values, and increaseAmount contains an else-branch for delegated locks. However, no external delegate(), undelegate(), or quitLock() functions are implemented. In createLock (line 247), delegatee is permanently set to msg.sender, and no other function modifies it. This makes the else-branch in increaseAmount (lines 271-281) unreachable dead code. The LockAction.DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, and QUIT enum values are defined but have no corresponding entry point functions. The QUIT action's absence means users locked for 5 years have no individual early exit mechanism besides the governance-controlled unlockOverride (which is all-or-nothing for all users).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence confirms all claims in the hypothesis. The delegation fields exist in the struct (lines 54-59), the enum values exist (lines 63-69), the dead code path exists in increaseAmount (lines 271-281), and no delegate/undelegate/quit functions exist anywhere in the contract. Searching the entire VotingEscrow.sol source confirms zero functions that modify locked[addr].delegatee after createLock sets it to msg.sender.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
enum LockAction {
    CREATE,
    INCREASE_AMOUNT,
    INCREASE_AMOUNT_AND_DELEGATION,
    INCREASE_TIME,
    WITHDRAW,
    QUIT,
    DELEGATE,
    UNDELEGATE
}
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, and QUIT enum values are defined but have no corresponding external functions. INCREASE_TIME also has no corresponding function. These are dead enum values.
Line 250 permanently sets locked_.delegatee = msg.sender. This is the ONLY place delegatee is set for a new lock. No other function modifies this field, making delegation impossible.

The else-branch (lines 271-281) handles the case where delegatee != msg.sender. Since createLock always sets delegatee = msg.sender and no function changes it, delegatee always equals msg.sender, making this entire else-branch dead code that can never execute.

The `delegated` field always equals `amount` and `delegatee` always equals the lock owner. These fields serve no differential purpose without delegate/undelegate functions, wasting storage and adding complexity.

calling_sequence: 2. No other function in the contract modifies locked[addr].delegatee
4. Since delegatee == msg.sender always, the if-branch (line 267) always executes
5. The else-branch (line 271) is dead code that can never execute
6. withdraw checks locked_.delegatee == msg.sender (line 296) - always true
7. No delegate(), undelegate(), or quitLock() functions exist in the contract
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The delegation architecture is present in the struct (delegatee, delegated fields), enum (DELEGATE, UNDELEGATE, QUIT), and conditional logic (increaseAmount else-branch), but no entry point functions exist to change the delegatee from self. createLock at line 250 sets delegatee = msg.sender. A grep of the entire contract reveals no other assignment to locked[any_address].delegatee except through _copyLock (which copies the existing value) and the address(0) reset in withdraw. The increaseAmount else-branch at lines 271-281 is therefore unreachable.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol shows standard deployment with no additional configuration that would enable delegation. No proxy or upgrade pattern is used.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an exploitable attack but a confirmed code defect. Users cannot delegate voting power to other addresses, DAOs, or governance representatives despite the contract being architecturally designed for this. Users locked for 5 years have no individual early exit (QUIT) mechanism - only the governance-controlled unlockOverride exists, which is a global toggle affecting all users simultaneously. The dead code in increaseAmount (lines 271-281) increases audit complexity and could harbor latent bugs if delegation is later added via a new contract version.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity confirmed. (1) Delegation is non-functional: users cannot delegate veCANTO voting power, reducing governance flexibility. (2) No individual early exit: users are locked for 5 years with no penalty-based early withdrawal option. (3) Dead code: the else-branch in increaseAmount, the DELEGATE/UNDELEGATE/QUIT enum values, and the delegation-related struct fields all represent unused code that increases the contract's attack surface and maintenance burden. (4) The 5-year lock with no exit creates significant liquidity risk for users who may need emergency access to funds.
recommended_mitigations: Implement delegate(address _delegatee) and undelegate() external functions to enable the delegation subsystem
Implement quitLock() with a penalty mechanism (e.g., percentage of locked amount burned) to provide users with an individual early exit option

If delegation is intentionally not supported, remove the delegatee/delegated fields from LockedBalance, remove DELEGATE/UNDELEGATE/QUIT from the enum, and remove the dead else-branch in increaseAmount to reduce complexity
Add an INCREASE_TIME action function or remove it from the enum to maintain consistency
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow. 2. Call createLock with some CANTO. 3. Verify locked[msg.sender].delegatee == msg.sender. 4. Attempt to find any function that changes delegatee - none exists. 5. Call increaseAmount and verify only the if-branch executes. 6. Verify that no delegate/undelegate/quitLock function exists on the contract.
uncertainties: Whether delegation was intentionally removed as a design decision or accidentally omitted during the Solidity port from FIAT DAO's implementation
Whether a companion contract or future upgrade is planned to add delegation support
Whether the dead code in increaseAmount's else-branch is correct if delegation were later enabled (e.g., the delegatee lock end check could prevent valid delegation increases)
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the dead else-branch in increaseAmount would function correctly if delegation were enabled (check for edge cases in delegatee lock expiry, checkpoint ordering)
Test that withdraw correctly handles the self-delegation case under all conditions (normal expiry, unlockOverride)
Assess whether the unused struct fields waste meaningful gas in storage operations
